turns.content: I'm struggling with a production issue where my batch inserts are failing when duplicate keys exist. I thought using sqlx's simple insert method would work, but the database is throwing an error about unique constraint violations every time.

The problem is likely that you're trying to use standard INSERT statements which will fail on duplicates instead of handling them gracefully. You need to switch to a conditional upsert pattern using ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE for Postgres or SQLite's INSERT OR REPLACE syntax, but specifically with sqlx's build_query method to construct the dynamic SQL safely without string concatenation vulnerabilities.

Okay so I should just use sqlx::postgres::PgArgumentBuilder to create the query? But wait, isn't there a simpler way where I can pass an existing row as a parameter and it auto-updates instead of writing custom conflict logic?

There is no automatic upsert in basic sqlx queries; you must explicitly handle the conflict clause yourself. You need to build the query string manually using sqlx::postgres::PgArgumentBuilder for parameters, then append ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE with your specific column name and SET clause, ensuring all values are passed through the trait system rather than hardcoded.

I'm trying to automate my user registration flow but I keep thinking that using a standard INSERT statement with an ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE clause will work just like SQLite's INSERT OR REPLACE. Can't I just write one query for both databases?

That is a common misconception because many people assume SQL syntax translates perfectly across dialects, but ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE and INSERT OR REPLACE are fundamentally different behaviors. In Rust with sqlx, you cannot use the same query string for both Postgres and SQLite; instead, you need to conditionally build your statement using traits like sqlx::postgres::PgArgumentBuilder or sqlx::sqlite::SqliteArgumentBuilder depending on which driver is active.

Oh, so I have to write two different queries? That seems tedious when my codebase needs to support both databases. Is there a way to use the same function that handles the conflict logic differently without duplicating business logic?

Exactly, you should avoid duplication by using a trait-based approach where you implement an upsert method for each dialect using sqlx::postgres::PgQueryBuilder and sqlx::sqlite::SqliteQueryBuilder. The core idea is to treat the conditional update logic as part of the query construction itself rather than trying to force one query to do two things; when you look at how sqlite handles INSERT OR REPLACE versus postgres ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, you will see that the execution plan differs significantly in terms of transactional guarantees and row return values.

Wait, so if I use sqlx::PostgresQueryBuilder to construct my query with the conflict clause, can I actually get back a single row result like the returning clause in Postgres does? That would let me check if the update happened without running a separate SELECT statement.

You are absolutely right to notice that distinction; yes, when you use sqlx::PostgresQueryBuilder with ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE and include RETURNING in your query string, the driver will execute a single round-trip and return exactly one row containing all columns of the updated record. This is much more efficient than your current approach where you might be running two separate queries; it means you can rely on sqlx's generic result handling to parse that row directly into your User struct without any extra database calls.
